expectations.text: Output contains all four MGPC sections: Mission, Goals, Premises, and Constraints as distinct labeled sections
expectations.evidence: requirements.md contains four clearly labeled sections: '### M — Mission', '### G — Goals', '### P — Premises', and '### C — Constraints', each with substantive content.

expectations.text: Mission or Premises captures that Godot is free/open-source and community-driven (not just 'a game engine')
expectations.evidence: Mission states: 'Democratize interactive media creation by giving every developer — regardless of resources, geography, or commercial backing — the means to build, distribute, and own professional-quality 2D and 3D games without restriction.' G3 adds 'Remain completely free and MIT-licensed with zero royalties and full source ownership' and G4 adds 'Be community-driven and independently governed through the Godot Foundation.' P2 covers community sustainability. The open-source and community-driven nature is woven throughout.

expectations.text: Goals section mentions cross-platform export (e.g. desktop, mobile, web) as a key capability goal
expectations.evidence: G2 explicitly states: 'Enable one-click cross-platform export to all major desktop, mobile, web, and console targets' with evidence cited from the README.

expectations.text: Constraints section includes the MIT license or equivalent open-source licensing as a hard constraint
expectations.evidence: C-H2 states: 'All core code must be MIT-licensed. Third-party libraries must be compatible; incompatible licenses require isolation.' Sourced from LICENSE.txt and FAQ.

expectations.text: The spec reflects game-engine-specific concerns (scripting language, editor, 2D/3D, physics, audio) — not generic software requirements
expectations.evidence: The spec explicitly covers: GDScript/C#/GDExtension scripting stack (G6, L5), unified 2D and 3D editor (G1), physics simulation (L5 topics), audio system (L5 topics), rendering pipeline with Forward+/Mobile/Compatibility renderers (L4 area), XR/AR/VR (L5), scene-node architecture (L5), and the 'dogfood editor' design (G7). These are all game-engine-specific concerns with no generic enterprise/web-app framing.

expectations.text: At least one non-obvious requirement is inferred (e.g. GDScript design goals, no royalties constraint, community governance implications)
expectations.evidence: Multiple non-obvious requirements are surfaced: C-H3 (no C++ exceptions — binary size and crash avoidance rationale inferred from FAQ), C-H4 (no STL — memory tracking and debug binary size), C-H5 (no ECS — inheritance-based model is a hard constraint, not just a preference), C-H7 (fully reproducible builds, no network downloads at build time), C-H6 (editor usable without admin privileges), and the GDExtension stable ABI rationale in the Solution Space table. These go well beyond what the README surface text says.

claims.claim: Extracted from GitHub repo README, CONTRIBUTING.md, godot-docs about/introduction.rst, about/faq.rst, about/list_of_features.rst, about/system_requirements.rst
claims.evidence: The header asserts this sourcing but there is no transcript to verify which tools were actually called. The content is highly consistent with those sources (e.g., exact quotes from the README are cited inline), making the claim plausible but not independently verifiable from outputs alone.

claims.claim: Method: requirements-extractor v2.0 (Phase 0 CoK saturation → Phase 1 MGPC intent inference)
claims.evidence: The document is structured in two labeled phases: Phase 0 (Bottom-Up CoK Saturation with L5→L1 layers) and Phase 1 (Top-Down Intent Inference with W-functor chain). The structure matches the claimed methodology.

claims.claim: The 'Why?' recursion chain terminates at a tautology / mission fixed point
claims.evidence: The W-functor chain in Phase 1 terminates with 'creative expression and making things is intrinsically valuable → TAUTOLOGY → Mission found', which is a defensible stopping criterion for the methodology.

claims.claim: All constraints are sourced with citations
claims.evidence: Each hard constraint row includes a 'Source' column with explicit citations (FAQ section names, LICENSE.txt, CONTRIBUTING.md). Soft constraints similarly cite source documents.

A 'Solution Space' section documents 7 design decision points (object model, scripting, GUI toolkit, build system, native ABI, rendering, core containers) with chosen approach, rejected alternatives, and rationale.

suggestions.assertion: Mission or Premises captures that Godot is free/open-source and community-driven (not just 'a game engine')
suggestions.reason: This passes even if the mention is shallow or buried in Goals rather than central to Mission framing. A tighter assertion would require the Mission statement itself (not just Goals/Premises) to explicitly ground the open-source/community angle — otherwise a spec that buries it in a footnote would also pass.

suggestions.assertion: At least one non-obvious requirement is inferred
suggestions.reason: The bar of 'at least one' is very easy to clear for any competent extraction. The skill produced 9 hard constraints, most non-obvious. A stronger assertion would specify a minimum count (e.g., 'at least three non-obvious constraints are inferred with source citations') or test for specific high-value inferences like the no-exceptions or no-STL constraints.

No assertion checks the Premises section specifically. The output's Premises are one of the highest-value parts (falsifying conditions, which Goal each premise supports) — this quality is completely unchecked by the current assertions. An assertion like 'Each premise is linked to the Goal(s) it supports and includes a falsifying condition' would meaningfully discriminate good from mediocre outputs.

No assertion checks that the spec cites sources for its claims. The output includes inline evidence for every Goal and constraint, which is high-quality behavior that goes completely unmeasured. A traceability assertion would catch hallucinated specs that have no source citations.

eval_feedback.overall: All six assertions pass and the output is genuinely high quality. The main weakness in the eval suite is that several assertions test presence rather than substance (open-source mentioned anywhere, one non-obvious req anywhere), and the Premises section — which is the most structurally distinctive part of a good MGPC spec — is not covered by any assertion.
